title: How TSMC Will Surpass 2nm and Other Insights from New IMEC CEO
gist: New IMEC CEO Patrick Vandenameele laid out four trends at ITF World: AI shifting to multi-agentic inference (a 150x compute increase), 'CMOS 2.0' vertical transistor stacking via wafer-to-wafer hybrid bonding (2nm-to-0.5nm plus stacking targeting a 10x performance gain over a decade), HBM/DRAM moving to the compute core, and silicon photonics replacing copper (100x interconnect efficiency), with TSMC's Kevin Zhang confirming FinFET-to-nanosheet-to-CFET scaling and a ~50x transistor-per-package increase.

themes.theme: AI demand shift from training to multi-agentic inference

insights.claim: IMEC CEO Patrick Vandenameele estimates that moving from AI training to multi-agentic inference will increase computing workloads 150-fold, as systems shift to dozens or hundreds of AI agents working together.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: A 150x inference-compute estimate underpins the entire hardware-scaling thesis; a bullish anchor for accelerator, memory, and interconnect demand.
insights.quote: Vandenameele estimates that moving from training to inference will increase computing workloads 150-fold.
insights.locator: Trend One: Demand from AI is Surging

insights.claim: The shift to multi-agentic AI fundamentally changes hardware requirements: memory and logic chips must sit physically closer together, and simply packing in more GPUs is no longer the optimal solution.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: Reinforces the shift from raw GPU count toward architecture and memory-logic proximity, favoring advanced packaging and 3D integration.
insights.quote: Simply packing in more GPUs is no longer the optimal solution; engineers must design the right computing architecture.
insights.locator: Trend One: Demand from AI is Surging

insights.claim: IMEC's 'CMOS 2.0' is a next-generation scaling platform that vertically stacks transistors with different functions across multiple layers connected through wafer-to-wafer hybrid bonding, with each layer optimized for a different function, rather than only shrinking transistors.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: Defines the post-planar scaling roadmap; hybrid bonding and 3D stacking become the value-capture layer, benefiting bonding-equipment and advanced-packaging players.
insights.quote: it stacks transistors with different functions vertically across multiple layers, connected through wafer-to-wafer hybrid bonding, with each layer optimized for a different function
insights.locator: Trend Two: Chip Scaling Enters the "CMOS 2.0" Era

insights.claim: IMEC's roadmap projects that shrinking chips from 2nm to 0.5nm combined with CMOS 2.0 stacking could deliver a tenfold performance improvement over the next decade, with even a two-layer stack delivering a significant energy-efficiency boost.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: A concrete 10x/decade performance roadmap and named node path (2nm to 0.5nm) for tracking scaling progress and capex direction.
insights.quote: shrinking chips from 2nm to 0.5nm combined with CMOS 2.0 stacking gains could deliver a tenfold performance improvement over the next decade

insights.claim: TSMC Co-COO Kevin Zhang confirmed transistor architecture is moving from FinFET to nanosheet with CFET as the next step, and that transistor scaling plus higher density plus 3D stacking could increase transistors per package nearly 50-fold in coming years.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: TSMC endorsing the CFET/3D path with a ~50x transistor-per-package figure signals where its leading-edge roadmap and packaging investment are heading.
insights.quote: the number of transistors within a single package could increase nearly 50-fold in coming years

insights.claim: TSMC's Kevin Zhang acknowledged the biggest current obstacle to stacking logic chips is that EDA tools do not yet support the approach, stating the whole ecosystem has to evolve in tandem.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: An explicit EDA-tooling gap is a bottleneck and an opportunity for EDA vendors (Synopsys/Cadence/Siemens) building 3D-stacking support.
insights.quote: the biggest current obstacle to stacking logic chips is that electronic design automation (EDA) tools don't yet support the approach

themes.theme: Memory moving to the compute core

insights.claim: Memory demand from agentic AI is growing explosively because each AI agent must continuously access and update its own status, and IMEC's architecture places high-bandwidth memory at the center of the compute module rather than at the chip edge.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: Memory-at-the-core reduces data-access energy and time, structurally raising HBM content and favoring near-memory-compute architectures.
insights.quote: IMEC's new architecture instead places high-bandwidth memory (HBM) at the center of the compute module.
insights.locator: Trend Three: Memory Is Moving Toward the Core

insights.claim: TSMC is working with DRAM partners to develop technology that stacks DRAM directly atop advanced logic chips, and Kevin Zhang cited Cerebras (which IPO'd this year) integrating more than 50 compute chips on a single wafer paired with large SRAM to accelerate inference as an early example of memory moving to the core.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: DRAM-on-logic stacking and the Cerebras wafer-scale example flag concrete near-memory-compute commercialization; watch DRAM makers partnering with TSMC.
insights.quote: Cerebras worked with TSMC to integrate more than 50 compute chips on a single wafer, paired with large amounts of high-speed SRAM to accelerate inference

insights.claim: As the number of chips in a rack rises, copper cabling is hitting bandwidth and physical-space limits, with Vandenameele noting the cables are already too big to fit in the rack, and the solution is silicon photonics using light instead of electricity.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: A physical-limit driver for optical interconnect adoption; copper's space/bandwidth ceiling is the catalyst for silicon-photonics/CPO demand.
insights.quote: The cables are already too big to fit in the rack.
insights.locator: Trend Four: Photonic Links Will Replace Copper

insights.claim: IMEC's roadmap aims to push optical connections from between racks to within racks and eventually to an 'optical highway' inside the interposer itself, a goal requiring more than 100-fold improvement in interconnect efficiency via coordinated breakthroughs in materials science, device design, and 3D integration.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: Optics-in-the-interposer is the long-run endgame for CPO; the 100x efficiency target sets the bar and the materials/3D dependencies to monitor.
insights.quote: eventually achieving what is called an "optical highway" inside the interposer itself
insights.locator: Trend Four: Photonic Links Will Replace Copper

themes.theme: IMEC's roadmap-setting role and industry convergence

insights.claim: IMEC does not make chips but defines the roadmap the global semiconductor industry follows, with TSMC, Samsung, Intel, and ASML as long-time partners and a technology roadmap already extending into the early 2040s.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: IMEC's roadmap functions as an industry leading indicator; its trend calls foreshadow where foundry and equipment capex will concentrate.
insights.quote: doesn't make chips—but it defines the roadmap the global semiconductor industry follows

insights.claim: TSMC Co-COO Kevin Zhang credited IMEC's pioneering research in new materials and new transistor architectures as a guiding beacon the industry follows toward mass production.
insights.speaker: B
insights.so_what: TSMC's public deference to IMEC underscores the institute's outsized influence on the leading-edge roadmap.
